activities_projects.title: Timurid Identity vs. Mongol Label
activities_projects.objective: Analyze why the Mughals emphasized their paternal lineage
steps: Contrast the legacy of Genghis Khan (associated with massacres) and Timur (the conqueror of Delhi in 1398)
Explain why the Mughals felt proud of their Timurid ancestry
Research the 'Timurid' group portrait mentioned in the text
Discuss how the name 'Mughal' is actually a derivative of 'Mongol', which they themselves didn't prefer
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the importance of historical legacy and identity in royal branding
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in explaining the preference for Timurid lineage over Mongol (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Mansabdari System Audit
activities_projects.objective: Understand the administrative link between rank, salary, and military duty
steps: Define the 'Mansabdar' as a grading system used by the Mughals
Differentiate between 'Zat' (personal status/salary) and 'Sawar' (number of cavalrymen required)
Explain why Akbar kept the 'Zat' and 'Sawar' nearly equal, and how this changed under Aurangzeb
Discuss the 'branding' of horses (dagh) to prevent corruption in the army
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the military and civil bureaucracy of the Mughal state
activities_projects.evaluation: Technical accuracy in defining the components of the Mansabdari system (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Zabt: The Science of Revenue
activities_projects.objective: Examine the economic reforms of Raja Todar Mal
steps: Explain the 10-year survey (1570–1580) of crop yields and prices
Describe how 'Zabt' worked in provinces where the land could be measured
Identify the role of 'Zamindars' as powerful local chieftains who collected this revenue
Analyze why the Zabt system was not possible in provinces like Gujarat and Bengal
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the sophisticated agricultural data collection used for state taxation
activities_projects.evaluation: Understanding of the statistical basis of Mughal revenue administration (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Abul Fazl’s Historiography
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the purpose of the 'Akbar Nama' and 'Ain-i Akbari'
steps: Identify the three volumes of 'Akbar Nama' (Ancestors, Reign, and Administration)
Explain why the 'Ain-i Akbari' is unique (it provides statistical details of crops, wages, and prices)
Discuss how Abul Fazl helped Akbar frame a vision of governance around 'Sulh-i Kul'
Contrast this official history with how a common peasant might have viewed the reign
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the importance of statistical and administrative records in historical writing
activities_projects.evaluation: Detailed description of the contents and purpose of the three volumes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Sulh-i Kul and the Ibadat Khana
activities_projects.objective: Explore Akbar's shift from orthodox Islam to universal peace
steps: Research the discussions at the 'Ibadat Khana' with Ulama, Brahmanas, Jesuit priests, and Zoroastrians
Define 'Sulh-i Kul' and its emphasis on ethics, honesty, and justice
Explain how this policy helped in governing a diverse country like India
Identify which later emperors (Jahangir and Shah Jahan) followed this policy
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the political necessity of religious tolerance in a multi-faith empire
activities_projects.evaluation: Conceptual clarity on the principles of Sulh-i Kul (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Jagirdari Crisis
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the economic reasons behind the decline of the Mughals
steps: Compare the assignment of 'Jagirs' under Akbar (carefully assessed) vs. Aurangzeb (shortage of land)
Explain how the increase in the number of Mansabdars led to a 'wait' for Jagirs
Analyze why Jagirdars tried to extract as much revenue as possible in a short time
Discuss how this system led to the oppression of the peasantry and rebellion
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the structural economic flaws that weakened the late Mughal Empire
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to link administrative expansion to economic instability (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Nur Jahan’s Political Sovereignty
activities_projects.objective: Examine the symbols of female power in the 17th century
steps: Identify the 'Farman' (Royal Order) issued by Nur Jahan with her square seal
Explain the significance of coins bearing the inscription 'Struck in the name of the Queen Begum, Nur Jahan'
Discuss her role in the 'Junta' (faction) that influenced Jahangir’s court
Write a summary on how Nur Jahan challenged the traditional role of royal women
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how women exercised direct political and economic authority in the Mughal court
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical interpretation of the artifacts of Nur Jahan’s rule (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mughal Relations with Other Rulers
activities_projects.objective: Trace the policy of matrimonial alliances and military pressure
steps: Explain the 'Rajput Policy' of the Mughals (marriages and high ranks)
Research the Sisodiya Rajputs’ refusal to accept Mughal suzerainty for a long time
Analyze how the Mughals treated defeated rulers (giving back land as 'Watan Jagir')
Discuss why Aurangzeb’s insult of Shivaji (1666) was a major political blunder
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the diplomatic strategies used to maintain an expansive empire
activities_projects.evaluation: Understanding of the 'Watan Jagir' concept and Rajput alliances (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Economic Contrast: 17th Century
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the gap between the elite and the producing classes
materials: Statistical data from Ain-i Akbari or modern research
steps: Calculate the percentage of revenue spent on just the top 5% of Mansabdars
Contrast the luxury of the Mughal court with the 'hand-to-mouth' existence of the peasantry
Explain why the primary producers (artisans/farmers) had little left for investment in tools
Research the 'famines' of the 17th century despite the empire's great wealth
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically evaluates the social inequality within the Mughal economic system
activities_projects.evaluation: Analysis of wealth distribution and its impact on production (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Succession Traditions: Primogeniture vs. Coparcenary
activities_projects.objective: Understand why Mughal successions were often violent
steps: Define 'Primogeniture' (eldest son inherits everything) and 'Coparcenary' (division of inheritance among all sons)
Identify which tradition the Mughals followed (Coparcenary/Timurid)
Explain how this led to civil wars between brothers (e.g., Aurangzeb vs. Dara Shukoh)
Discuss the impact of these wars on the stability of the empire
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains the legal and cultural reasons behind Mughal wars of succession
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct differentiation between the two types of inheritance (10 marks)
